data.title: DuPont (DD) Stock May Trade At A Discount Despite Lithium Extraction Launch
data.description:

At the same time, ongoing legal challenges linked to PFAS lawsuits may weigh on how much of that potential investors are willing to price in today.

Is DuPont de Nemours a Bargain on Cash Flow?

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) approach estimates what DuPont de Nemours is worth based on the cash it can generate for shareholders over time. On these assumptions, the DCF points to an intrinsic value of about $215.79 per share, compared with the current share price of $137.40, implying the stock screens as 36.3% undervalued. data.title: Apple (AAPL) Faces Samsung’s New Foldables As Foldable iPhone Expectations Build
data.description:
• Samsung has launched three new foldable smartphones, adding fresh competition in the premium device segment.
  • The launches come as Apple, ticker NasdaqGS:AAPL, is reported to be preparing its own foldable device entry.
  • The timing sets up a direct comparison between Samsung's current foldable line up and expectations for a potential foldable iPhone.

For investors watching Apple, the latest move from Samsung focuses attention on how a future foldable iPhone could fit into Apple's broader product line. Apple shares recently closed at $321.66, with the stock up 18.7% year to date and 51.1% over the past year. Returns of 68.8% over three years and 126.6% over five years show how closely the market tends to track shifts in Apple's hardware cycle.

The expectation that Apple may capture significant share of the foldable smartphone segment adds weight to how and when the company enters this category. Samsung's new devices effectively set a benchmark for pricing, features, and user experience that Apple will be measured against. For shareholders, the key question is how a foldable product might influence Apple's future device mix and longer term revenue profile.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Apple by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Apple.

Does the team leading Apple have what it takes? See our full breakdown of the management team's track record and compensation.

For Apple, the timing of Samsung’s three new foldable phones puts John Ternus’ early product decisions under the spotlight. Ternus has been closely linked to Apple’s hardware roadmap, so investors will be watching how a future foldable iPhone reflects his approach to balancing design, durability, and integration with Apple Intelligence and services. Samsung, Huawei and other Android handset makers already have multiple foldable generations in market, so Apple’s leadership team is effectively choosing to arrive later with a product that will be judged against a defined set of features, form factors and price points.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Apple Narrative

  • The expected foldable iPhone supports the existing Apple narrative that new device formats, paired with AI-powered features and a growing services ecosystem, can keep users inside the Apple hardware and software loop for longer.
  • Samsung’s head start in foldables, together with strong efforts from Huawei and Xiaomi, challenges the assumption that Apple can rely solely on ecosystem strength without matching or exceeding competitors’ hardware experiments.
  • The narrative around Apple focuses heavily on AI assistants, services and emerging markets, but it does not fully address how leadership under John Ternus might handle higher hardware complexity, repairability and long-term support for foldable devices.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Apple to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ A misstep on foldable hardware under new leadership, whether through reliability issues or a design that fails to stand out against Samsung and Huawei, could raise questions about Apple’s product-execution track record.
  • ⚠️ Entering the foldable category at premium pricing without clear differentiation could compress unit volumes or push customers toward cheaper Android alternatives, especially if repair costs or durability concerns surface.
  • 🎁 A carefully timed foldable launch that integrates tightly with Apple Intelligence, services and wearables could reinforce Apple’s ecosystem and give Ternus early credibility as a product-focused CEO.
  • 🎁 If Apple uses its late entry to address pain points seen in early foldables, such as hinge reliability and screen longevity, it could strengthen perceptions of quality and help sustain high-end iPhone demand.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, watch how Apple under John Ternus talks about foldables on earnings calls and product events, especially any comments on where a foldable iPhone would sit in the existing line up. Pay attention to how quickly Samsung iterates on its new Galaxy Z Fold and Z Flip models, and whether Apple references those devices when framing its own choices. It is also worth tracking investor and user reaction to early leaks or prototypes, as this will offer clues on whether Apple is prioritizing ultra-premium pricing, mass-market appeal, or a gradual, lower-volume introduction into the foldable category.

Over recent periods, the company has seen quarterly revenue move from €420.4 million and EPS of €0.61 in Q2 2025 to €479.6 million and EPS of €0.69 in Q2 2026, while trailing twelve month EPS increased from €2.12 to €2.71, and these results sit alongside a reported net profit margin of 9.5% for the latest trailing period. With earnings over the last year up 27% and margins higher than the prior year, the latest release puts profitability and margin resilience at the center of the Kalmar Oyj story.

See our full analysis for Kalmar Oyj.

With the numbers on the table, the next step is to see how Kalmar Oyj's recent performance lines up with the prevailing narratives around its growth, risks, and profitability, and where those storylines might need updating.

See what the community is saying about Kalmar Oyj

Advertisement

Margins and LTM earnings shift support the bullish Kalmar Oyj story

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, Kalmar Oyj reports net profit of €173.4 million on €1.8b of revenue, with net profit margin at 9.5% compared with 8.1% a year earlier and earnings up 27% over that period.
  • Bulls highlight that this 27% earnings rise and higher margin fit with their view that profitability can improve further, yet the earlier five year earnings decline of 6.6% per year keeps the historical record mixed and
    • the bullish view references margin expansion assumptions, which sit alongside the already reported move to a 9.5% net margin over the last 12 months,
    • while the long run decline in earnings growth means the recent trailing strength needs to be weighed against what has been a weaker multi year trend.
For investors who want to see how bullish analysts connect these margin trends to their long term expectations, their full case for Kalmar Oyj is set out in the 🐂 Kalmar Oyj Bull Case.

Order and pricing risks keep bears focused on earnings durability

  • Across the last 12 months, earnings rose 27% while net profit margin moved to 9.5%, yet over the past five years average earnings still declined 6.6% per year, which is what cautious investors tend to focus on when asking whether the latest improvement can last.
  • Bears argue that factors such as weaker equipment orders in some periods, heavy reliance on price increases of around 5% to 15% and tariff exposure could limit how long margins stay near current levels, even with the margin at 9.5% and the order book referred to at about €1b, and
    • this concern links directly to the multi year earnings decline figure of 6.6% per year, which shows that prior profitability did not follow a straight upward path,
    • while the recent 27% earnings rebound tests the more cautious view by showing that, at least over the most recent year, profit levels have recovered from that longer period of weaker results.
Skeptics who want to see how these risks are laid out in full can walk through the detailed cautious case for Kalmar Oyj in the 🐻 Kalmar Oyj Bear Case.

Valuation gap stands out against DCF fair value and analyst target

  • Kalmar Oyj trades at €37.78 with a P/E of 13.9x, while the DCF fair value is cited at €65.11 and the analyst price target at €43.80, and that P/E sits below the European Machinery industry at 21.6x and peers at 22.4x.
  • Supporters of the bullish narrative point to this valuation gap and the 9.5% net margin and 27% trailing earnings rise as reasons the stock could be pricing in the weaker five year trend of 6.6% annual earnings decline and below market forecast growth of about 9% per year for earnings and 4.5% per year for revenue, rather than the more optimistic margin and profit assumptions they model, and
    • the contrast between the €37.78 share price and both the €65.11 DCF fair value and €43.80 analyst target is central to that argument,
    • while the lower 13.9x P/E compared with industry and peer multiples highlights how much the market is currently discounting the company relative to those reference points.

Next Steps

If the mix of bullish and cautious takes on Kalmar Oyj feels split, use the full data set to move quickly and form your own stance. Begin with the 4 key rewards for a detailed starting point.

See What Else Is Out There

data.title: Is Caterpillar (CAT) Reasonably Valued After Its 38% Sales Jump?
data.description:
Caterpillar stock has delivered a very large 5 year return, yet its current valuation signals are mixed, with the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) intrinsic value estimate suggesting the shares are roughly in line with fair value, while the market multiple checks lean more supportive.

  • Over 5 years, Caterpillar has returned about 3.6x an initial investment, which puts extra focus on whether the current price still leaves room for a reasonable margin of safety.
  • The push to grow higher margin, recurring revenue from Construction Industries and related digital solutions can support expectations for future cash flows. At the same time, softer equipment demand and concerns about dealer inventory and margins may weigh on how much investors are willing to pay for those cash flows.
  • Caterpillar currently passes only 1 of 6 valuation checks. This points to a stock that does not screen as a clear bargain on the broader tests, even with a supportive multiple read.

The issue now is whether Caterpillar's current share price already reflects this mix of strong past returns and balanced fair value signals, or if the recent run still leaves scope for mispricing to emerge.

Caterpillar delivered 110.1% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Machinery industry.

Advertisement

Does Caterpillar Look Fairly Valued on Cash Flow?

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates what Caterpillar’s future cash generation could be worth in today’s dollars. Caterpillar is producing solid cash generation, with latest twelve month free cash flow of about $8.5b and a DCF built on growing cash flows over time. On these inputs, the model points to an estimated intrinsic value of about $814 per share.

With the current share price sitting roughly 9.9% above that estimate, the DCF output suggests Caterpillar stock is slightly overvalued rather than a clear bargain. The recent 38% increase in Construction Industries sales, and the push toward more recurring revenue, helps explain why the market is willing to price the shares above what the cash flow model supports today.

Overall, the cash flow picture and current price point to Caterpillar being about fairly valued on this DCF view, rather than strongly undervalued or clearly overvalued.

Caterpillar is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Caterpillar.

Is Caterpillar a Bargain on Earnings?

The P/E ratio is a common way to value a company like Caterpillar because earnings are a key driver of how investors think about industrial stocks. Caterpillar currently trades at about 43.7x earnings, compared with the Machinery industry average of roughly 27.9x and a peer group average of about 30.6x.

On a more tailored view that considers Caterpillar’s size, margins, sector and risks, the fair P/E ratio is estimated at around 52.2x. That is higher than the current 43.7x, which indicates the stock trades at a discount to what this framework suggests could be reasonable, even though it already sits above broader industry and peer benchmarks.

Taken together, Caterpillar stock appears undervalued on this P/E multiple approach despite its premium to the industry and peer averages.

See what the numbers say about this price — find out in our valuation breakdown.

The Caterpillar Narrative: What Would Justify Today's Price?

Simply Wall St Narratives pick up where the Caterpillar valuation puzzle leaves off. They spell out which assumptions about Caterpillar's future growth, margins and earnings would need to hold for the stock to be worth meaningfully more or less than today's price. Each one sets out fair value as a thesis about the business that you can watch over time, rather than a single static snapshot, and they are available on Simply Wall St's Community page.

Community views on Caterpillar sit far apart, with some investors seeing upside left and others arguing it is already priced for perfection.

Bull case: 8% undervalued

"Record backlog growth across all three primary segments, driven by strong global infrastructure demand (particularly in North America, Africa, and the Middle East), positions Caterpillar for above-trend sales growth in late 2025 and into 2026, supporting top-line revenue expansion…"

Read the full Bull Case to see why Caterpillar could be undervalued

Bear case: 62% overvalued

"Historically, CAT trades at lower multiples. The current premium reflects optimism about global infrastructure spending and the mining supercycle…"

Read the full Bear Case to see why Caterpillar could be overvalued

Do you think there's more to the story for Caterpillar? Head over to our Community to see what others are saying!

The Bottom Line

For Caterpillar, the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) intrinsic value estimate points to a stock that is roughly in line with fair value, while the tailored P/E framework suggests it trades on an undervalued multiple. That split reflects tension between cash flow assumptions on one side and investor expectations baked into earnings multiples on the other, especially after such an extreme move in the share price. Broader valuation checks remain weak, so the key question from here is whether Caterpillar can sustain the level of earnings and margins implied by today’s multiple without leaving investors exposed to a value trap.

data.title: Defensive Dividend Stocks For Higher Oil Prices And Sticky Inflation
data.description:
With oil prices climbing, Treasury yields near 4.71% and investors worrying about inflation and interest rates staying higher for longer, dividend stocks with steadier business models are back in focus. This article looks at how the latest headlines around the Iran war, bond markets and Fed expectations connect to a Defensive Dividend Stocks screener, and what that might mean for investors who care about income and resilience when markets are under pressure. Ahead, you will see 3 stocks from this screener that appear positively exposed to these developments.

Advertisement

Atmos Energy (ATO)

Overview: Atmos Energy is a regulated natural gas utility based in Dallas that distributes gas to about 3.4 million customers across eight US states. It also operates pipelines and underground storage that move and store gas for itself and third parties.

Operations: Atmos Energy generates most of its US$5.7b business revenue from its Distribution segment at about US$4.6b. The Pipeline and Storage segment contributes around US$1.1b.

Market Cap: US$29.8b

Atmos Energy stands out in the Defensive Dividend Stocks screener because it combines a long record of regular dividend increases with a regulated utility model that can appeal when rates and inflation are in focus. The company is investing heavily in modern pipelines and storage, supported by regulatory frameworks that are designed to help recover those costs. Demand from data centers and industrial users supports its gas infrastructure business. At the same time, earnings growth, a P/E close to estimated fair levels and an 85.4% discount to Simply Wall St’s fair value estimate raise valuation questions that income investors tend to care about. However, higher debt use, weaker free cash flow coverage of the dividend and legal and regulatory risks mean you need to look more closely at how durable this cash return story really is.

Atmos Energy’s regulated cash flows and the large gap to fair value raise a simple question: is the market mispricing this dividend story or sensing something bigger in the background? Find out in the DCF valuation analysis for Atmos Energy

Otter Tail (OTTR)

Overview: Otter Tail Corporation is a Fergus Falls based company that combines a regulated electric utility serving customers in Minnesota, North Dakota and South Dakota with manufacturing operations and a plastics business that produces PVC pipes for water, wastewater and other infrastructure uses across multiple end markets.

Operations: Otter Tail generates around US$582.9m of revenue from its Electric segment, US$408.4m from Plastics and US$322.4m from Manufacturing.

Market Cap: US$3.9b

Otter Tail provides a mix of regulated utility earnings and industrial exposure, which can be appealing when rising oil prices, higher Treasury yields and inflation concerns are putting pressure on growth stocks. The utility segment focuses on grid reliability and renewables, while the plastics and manufacturing businesses are directly exposed to PVC resin and broader industrial demand, so the Iran war and resin price moves matter. At the same time, investors may consider the impact of higher rates on its debt funded capital plan and the ongoing PVC Pipe Antitrust Litigation settlements in the context of its long history of paying dividends and relatively low P/E versus peers. The overall picture depends on how these factors interact over the next few years.

Otter Tail’s mix of regulated power and PVC driven plastics is harder to read than a typical utility, and that is exactly why the analysis report for Otter Tail might change how you see its dividend story

National Fuel Gas (NFG)

Overview: National Fuel Gas is a diversified US energy company that combines natural gas exploration and production, interstate pipelines and storage, and a regulated gas utility serving customers in New York and Pennsylvania. Its integrated model links the wells that produce gas to the networks that transport, store and deliver it to utilities, industrial users, power plants and households.

Operations: National Fuel Gas generates around US$1.3b of revenue from Integrated Upstream and Gathering, about US$930.5m from its Utility segment and roughly US$429.8m from Pipeline and Storage, with small corporate eliminations.

Market Cap: US$7.9b

National Fuel Gas sits at the intersection of energy production, critical infrastructure and regulated utility income. This mix can be appealing when higher oil prices, rising borrowing costs and inflation worries are affecting growth stocks. The company combines a long history of dividends with a P/E below both the US market and its gas utility peers, while recent results show solid profitability and a higher quarterly payout. At the same time, it carries meaningful debt, faces higher diesel and project costs linked to the Iran conflict, and operates in regions where tighter climate policy could affect long term gas demand. The key question for investors is how its integrated structure, hedging approach and planned expansion projects balance those risks against the income and value characteristics of the business.

National Fuel Gas appears to be a classic income stock on the surface, but its integrated model, debt load and project pipeline could be masking a very different risk reward profile in the 5 key rewards and 1 important warning sign

Price-to-Earnings of 20.8x: Is it justified?

On a P/E of 20.8x, ORIX JREIT trades at a level that suggests the market is willing to pay a premium for each unit of current earnings, with the last close at ¥101,700 and a 1 year total return of 10.29%.

The P/E multiple compares the current unit price with earnings per unit. It reflects what investors are paying today for the trust's existing profit stream. For a diversified Japanese REIT like ORIX JREIT, which focuses on income producing properties across offices, logistics, retail, residential, and hotels, this is a common yardstick for how the market is pricing its income engine.

There are some mixed signals. On one hand, earnings have grown by 4.9% per year over the past 5 years, with a stronger 19.4% earnings growth over the past year and higher net profit margins at 45.2% compared with 43.6% previously. This helps explain why ORIX JREIT screens as good value against its peer average P/E of 23.2x. On the other hand, the P/E of 20.8x is higher than the broader Asian Office REITs industry average of 14.2x. This means the current valuation assumes a stronger earnings profile than many regional peers despite debt that is not well covered by operating cash flow and funding that is entirely reliant on borrowings rather than deposits.
